column7: The depth of functionality is impressive – we cover general ledger, AP/AR, inventory, and reporting all from one platform without needing third-party add-ons for the basics. The integration with the rest of the Microsoft ecosystem (Outlook, Excel, Power BI) is genuinely useful in day-to-day work, especially being able to pull data straight into Excel and push edits back. Role-based dashboards help keep people focused on what's relevant to them, and the dimensional accounting setup gives us flexibility to slice financial data in ways that would be painful in smaller systems.
column8: The user interface still feels dated in places and navigation can be clunky when you're jumping between modules. Initial setup and configuration require real expertise – it's not something a small team can spin up without a partner or consultant. Performance can slow down on heavier reports, and customization often pushes you toward AL development rather than simple configuration. Documentation exists but is scattered, so finding the right answer sometimes takes longer than it should.
column10: It gives us a single source of truth for financial and operational data, which removed a lot of the spreadsheet juggling we used to rely on. Month-end close is faster and more auditable, intercompany transactions are easier to manage, and having AP, AR, and GL in one place means fewer reconciliation headaches. The reporting layer, once set up properly, lets finance produce numbers without constantly chasing other departments for data. Powerful

One of the newer things I really like about Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is how much focus they’ve started putting on AI and automation.

Recently, features like AI-powered agents (for things like sales orders and payables) are really interesting. They can automate repetitive tasks like processing emails or matching invoices, which saves a lot of manual effort and reduces errors. It feels like the system is becoming more proactive rather than just a tracking tool.
column8:

column7: Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central offers seamless integration across finance, operations, sales, and inventory management — all in one centralized platform. I really appreciate its user-friendly interface, real-time data insights, and the flexibility to customize workflows according to business needs. The fact that it connects effortlessly with other Microsoft tools like Excel, Outlook, and Power BI makes it even more efficient for day-to-day operations. It simplifies complex business processes and helps improve decision-making with accurate, real-time information.
column8: While Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is a powerful and comprehensive solution, it can sometimes feel complex for new users, especially during the initial setup and configuration stages. The customization and integration process may require technical expertise or support from a partner, which can increase implementation time and cost. Additionally, report customization options could be more intuitive, as creating tailored reports sometimes involves advanced knowledge of Power BI or extensions.
column10: Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central helps solve challenges related to manual data handling, disconnected systems, and lack of real-time visibility across departments. It centralizes financials, inventory, sales, and operations into one platform, allowing for better collaboration and faster decision-making. Automation of routine processes reduces errors and saves time, while built-in analytics provide clear insights into business performance. column7: I really like Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central's seamless integration with all the tools, especially in the Microsoft ecosystem. The SaaS deployment is great as it removes the need for infrastructure management, patching, and upgrade projects, letting teams focus completely on deliverability and value creation. The financials have a solid and simple structure, which I find reliable with strong governance through posting logic, dimensions, and audit trails. The personalized dashboards and Power BI integration allow for clean, crisp, and beautiful dashboards. AL extensions and APIs enable customization while protecting the base application with future updates. It also supports organizations as they mature, without forcing an early move, which I appreciate.
column8: There are a few product aspects of Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central that could be improved. One issue is that its simplicity is sometimes too much, and it needs a bit more for users who want more functionality. Managing multiple customizations and ISVs along with updates becomes cumbersome. Operational users often struggle, as the UI, while logical, is not always intuitive for warehousing. Advanced manufacturing and warehousing often require third-party apps. The standard reports are adequate but very limited, requiring a lot of customizations. Integrations are complex, and Microsoft updates, while beneficial, can disrupt customizations. High transaction values can bottleneck the system, causing performance degradation. Early design decisions like COA dimensions and posting groups are hard to reverse and can impact long-term usability if rushed.

column7: What I genuinely appreciate most about Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is how smoothly everything connects across the business. Finance, inventory, sales, and reporting are all in one location and updated instantly, eliminating the need to switch between various tools. It saves a lot of headaches. I also adore how user-friendly the interface is; it doesn't take long to figure out where things are, and the dashboards genuinely give you a quick overview of what's happening in the company. Additionally, exporting data or working with the team is a breeze thanks to the integration with other Microsoft products like Excel, Teams, and Power BI. The implementation was smoother than expected, customer support has been dependable, and we now use the system every day across the business.
column8:
The biggest issue, if I'm being completely honest, is that some aspects of the system seem a little too "Microsoft-structured." To put it another way, you can accomplish nearly anything, but occasionally it requires more clicks or navigation than you might anticipate. Some processes aren't as simple as they ought to be.

Additionally, despite the system's strength, customization can be challenging without technical assistance. Simple adjustments are acceptable, but more complex ones typically require a partner or developer, which increases expenses and time.
column10:
Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central has helped us eliminate a lot of disconnected systems and manual processes that used to slow us down. Previously, the sales, inventory, and finance departments used different tools, which resulted in errors, duplication of effort, and delays. We all work from the same precise data because everything is now centralized and updated in real time.

Additionally, it has resolved our reporting issues. Rather than extracting data from various spreadsheets, I am able to swiftly produce comprehensible reports and genuinely believe the outcomes. Additionally, inventory management has improved; forecasting, orders, and stock levels are much simpler to manage, which lowers overspending and stock-outs. Seamless Business Integration and Effortless

column7:
Unified Platform
It connects finance, sales, purchasing, inventory, and operations in one centralized system—ideal for small to mid-sized businesses that want end-to-end visibility.

Integration with Microsoft 365
Business Central works effortlessly with Outlook, Excel, Teams, and SharePoint. You can create quotes or invoices directly from Outlook, analyze data in Excel, or collaborate on Teams without needing to switch systems.

Customizable and Scalable
It allows easy customization through extensions and offers scalability as your company grows. You can start with the essentials and add modules or apps later.
column8:
Steep Learning Curve
While the interface is modern, the system can feel overwhelming at first—especially for users without prior ERP experience. Initial training is usually required to navigate it effectively.

Limited Out-of-the-Box Reporting
The built-in reports often need customization to meet specific business needs. Many users end up relying on Power BI or custom report development, which may require IT or consultant support.
column10:
Disjointed Systems
Before Business Central, we used separate tools for accounting, inventory, and sales—which led to manual work, delays, and data inconsistencies. Business Central brings everything into one platform, eliminating duplication and improving accuracy.

Limited Real-Time Insights
We had difficulty accessing up-to-date financial and operational data quickly. Business Central’s real-time dashboards and integration with Power BI now give us immediate visibility into KPIs, helping leadership make faster, more informed decisions.

Manual Processes
Month-end close, approvals, and recurring transactions used to involve a lot of manual effort. Business Central automates many of these tasks, saving time and reducing the risk of human error. Older Software - Good for

column7: What stands out most with Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is how smoothly everything connects—from setup to daily use. It’s easy to get started thanks to helpful guidance and intuitive design, and once it’s live, the integration with tools like Excel, Outlook, and Power BI makes everyday tasks feel familiar and efficient. The customer support has been responsive when needed, and the sheer range of features—especially for finance, inventory, and reporting—means we use it frequently and productively. It’s packed with functionality, yet doesn’t overwhelm, which is rare for an ERP.
column8: Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central has a lot going for it, but there are a few areas that can trip you up. Some advanced configurations feel complex, and getting third-party integrations to play nicely isn’t always plug-and-play. The performance can lag a bit with larger datasets, which isn’t ideal when speed matters. And while the UI is generally user-friendly, certain modules aren’t as intuitive as others—so you may end up spending more time training or troubleshooting than expected. It’s a powerful system, but it does ask for some patience.
column10: Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central helps tackle the everyday chaos that can come with managing finance, inventory, and customer operations across a growing organization. It centralizes data from different departments, which means less jumping between systems and fewer errors from manual entry. For us, it’s solved headaches around tracking financials, managing stock, and keeping customer orders flowing smoothly—giving us real-time insights and a clearer picture of how the business is performing. The benefit? Less firefighting, more forward-planning. It’s streamlined the way we work, so we can focus on outcomes instead of troubleshooting. Harnessing Business Efficiency with Microsoft Dynamics 365

column7: As an integrations specialist, I've enjoyed the straight-forward approach to a Business Process. I'm a daily user, both in front and back end. I've implemented everything a Small/Med Size business could need, and rarely depended on a service provider to make-sense of what I was doing. column8: There is not much to dislike. Unlike other ERP's, Business Central relies on an intentional action-and-effect process for most workflows in a business. Because of this, user errors are almost always visible and hard to hide.

column10:
One of the most common problems with Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is the initial learning curve. Although the platform is powerful and comprehensive, it can be challenging at first to understand all its functionalities and how they integrate with each other, especially if you have no prior experience with ERP systems. Additionally, **customizations** can take time and resources to adapt them to the specific needs of your business.

How does that benefit me?

Despite the learning curve, the long-term benefit is enormous. Once you master the system, you have complete control over your business operations, from financial management to the supply chain. The integration between all the modules allows you to have a complete and updated view of what is happening, which improves decision-making. And although customizations can be costly and time-consuming, the flexibility to adapt it to the business needs is a great advantage. Cómo Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central Hizo Mi Trabajo

column7: In my former role at Croc, I was deeply involved in developing and testing the functionality of WMS modules in an ERP system, which mirrors the kind of integration prowess Dynamics 365 BC offers. The platform’s flexibility allowed for efficient project tracking and quality control, analogously to my collaboration with QA teams in rigorous regression testing and documentation practices.
column8:
While Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is a robust ERP platform, one potential drawback I've perceived is that the extensive functionality can sometimes lead to a complex learning curve for new users. This complexity can necessitate comprehensive training and adjustment period, an observation consistent with my experience at Rokiškio, where I trained and consulted over 50 employees on new systems and processes, underscoring the need to streamline the onboarding process.

Moreover, customizations, though beneficial for tailoring the system to specific business needs, can add additional complexity and require expert involvement. In my role at Croc, where I was involved in the development of WMS module functionality for an ERP system, I saw the necessity for striking a balance between system flexibility and usability.

Lastly, the cost associated with the implementation and maintenance of Business Central might be a barrier for smaller businesses. In my professional journey, especially when implementing cost optimization initiatives at TUI Travel, I've learned to weigh such investments carefully against anticipated ROI. This acumen in strategic analysis and implementation aids in making informed decisions when it comes to platforms like Dynamics 365 Business Central.
column10:
Operational Efficiency: Dynamics 365 Business Central streamlines various business processes into one comprehensive system, thereby enhancing the workflow efficiency. My experience leading cost optimization initiatives at TUI Travel aligns with this benefit, as I continuously seek ways to refine operational practices to drive substantial cost savings and productivity gains.

Data Centralization and Reporting: The platform provides a unified view of business data, aiding in better reporting and strategic decision-making. Having conducted advanced data set analyses and managed key performance metrics at TUI Travel, the benefit of Business Central's centralized data ecosystem has been indispensable for informed decision-making and identifying streams for revenue maximization.

CRM Integration: Dynamics 365 Business Central integrates CRM capabilities within its framework, allowing for improved customer engagement and sales efficiency—a direct correlation to the enhanced customer interaction I achieved with the 15% increase in engagement through the Salesforce CRM implementation.

column7:
Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central is an efficient and unified solution for project management and business operations. The platform provides advanced workflow management tools with collaboration capabilities. It is available and accessible via cloud-based, on-premises, and mobile apps for iOS and Android.

The user interface is well-organized with full customization capabilities, has built-in reporting capabilities, provides prebuilt apps via Microsoft AppSource, and integrates seamlessly with Power Automate, Power BI, and various Microsoft products, especially Office 365.

The service provides helpful learning resources via online forums, user groups, learning catalogs, analyst reports, and case studies.
column8: While MS Dynamics 365 Business Central integrates and operates seamlessly within the Microsoft ecosystem, it presents challenges to migrate business data and workflows to different platforms or solution providers.
column10:
Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central helps my team find the resources, information, financial data, and customer feedback related to our workflow all in one unified workspace. 

The service saves our time and allows us to collaborate on project planning and task management and to track overall work progress as we access it anytime from anywhere to keep along with the project's timeline. Also, the Power BI integration allows us to generate more detailed reports and build advanced analytics dashboards.

column7: Microsoft Dynamics 365 is a comprehensive business management solution with powerful features to increase productivity and profitability. It offers sophisticated reporting and analysis tools for forecasting, as well as the ability to effectively monitor the inputs and outputs of your business. Its versatile accounting foundation provides complete control over every aspect of general ledger-level operations from any type of organisation or area. In addition, its comprehensive human resources, manufacturing integration and supply chain planning capabilities all come together in an easy-to-use interface that users can access from anywhere, at any time.

column7: I thought the ERP system was user-friendly and easy to navigate. Being a Microsoft product, it is also easily integrated with Microsoft Excel (simple to copy and paste journal entries from Excel into the system). Being able to attach journal entry and A/P invoice support within the system as well were huge improvements for us. We also integrated our ERP system with external systems as well (Concur and VisualLease). We also utilized Jet Reports for our financial reporting as well as creating ad-hoc reporting.
column8: Nothing against the ERP system itself, but I heard the initial employee training we received from an outside vendor assisting with the implementation wasn't great. Fortunately, I had past experience with the system and was able to help train our employees on the system.
column9: We had upgraded from a less intuitive ERP system - since our company had many legal entities, the largest benefit was in terms of added efficiency with producing consolidated financial statements, which we accomplished through Jet Reports. The system, I thought, was much easier to use and had better controls over the posting of transactions in the system. We also implemented multi-company management to allow us to quickly and easily open and close the posting periods for all of our legal entities simultaneously rather than needing to update the posting period for each individual legal entity.
column10: I think it's a great product but make sure you have a subject-matter expert as an employee or engage with a skilled vendor to conduct employee training on the system for the best results.

column7: <v ->My name's Anthony Darden.</v> I'm Vice President IT of a global manufacturing company. And I give Business Central SaaS a nine out of 10. We are a global company, and have locations, both manufacturing, and sales, and service across the globe. So connecting those locations together is a challenge. And we want to be able to report through a single lens, and be able to have a view into the business regardless of the region. And the Business Central SaaS application allows us to do that without having to worry about infrastructure costs and maintaining kind of the back of the house IT elements of it. It's 100% cloud solution, so every region, every location, they're using the same version, and we can report up to corporate through that single lens which was our challenge to begin with. Favorite features about the product is how they embed Power BI, that's Microsoft flagship reporting platform, highly visual, really empowers the user to craft their own content. And it's easy to distribute and publish. So we find that very powerful and effective for our users across the world and around around the boardroom, 'cause there's high level reporting that we count on month in and month out. The other interest that we have is more geared toward the future. And that's with Cortana Intelligence. We have a tremendous amount of data out there, and how we leverage that in the future is one of those exciting things. When it comes to forecasting and planning, it's just the technology that we're going to stay very close to. And it starts with these Business Central implementations. Two things I'd like to see improved as Microsoft continues to build upon this product and extend it. One is a more simplified licensing model. From a corporate standpoint, we have five different international Business Central tenants. So for us to administer, we have to log in separately to each, that just lacks that simplified way to manage the solution. I know they'll get there, but certainly there's a lot of opportunity and growth potential there. The second thing is cost, pricing. It's easy to say that, you know, switching to the cloud will be a cost benefit to the end-user, but I find that the more ad-ons we tack on to satisfy a, what's the word, to satisfy a need we have, or a feature need, that's another monthly subscription costs that you tack on. So you got to be very careful. I feel like it can be more expensive for companies that are in our mid-market space, or mid-sized space I should say. So I just caution anybody to really do the calculations, to do the math before you make that leap. And I trust that Microsoft will start to embed more features into the core product so that we can lessen the reliance on having these third-party ad-ons that just add to our cost, add to our budget. So I used to be an early skeptic of committing to the cloud 100%. So a couple points of advice I'd give to those considering. One, the performance is very good. We have locations in Australia, Europe, China, Canada, Mexico, and I can tell you that the user experience in each of those regions has been very favorable. So I was always a skeptic that with the cloud comes inconsistent performance. I can tell you that it gets better all the time, and I just see that continues to improve. But adding on to that point, it does shift your investment dollars. Instead of maintaining an internal infrastructure and team, I would focus on connectivity, having multiple internet providers pooled as one into your facility. Because the more you commit to the cloud, the more important it is to maintain that connection to the cloud. So having those types of redundancies is important. And three, your users will embrace it. We try to model everything against the Amazon experience. So if you're wondering how your users will embrace a switch to a cloud solution like Business Central SaaS, I can tell you that has been overwhelmingly positive. Both for old-school legacy employees and new age. Has a very modern user interface. The mobile capabilities keep getting better, and how they leverage Cortana Intelligence in the future is one of those exciting things that are coming, so I don't have any reservations about making the switch. And

column7: Out of the box, Great Plains (GP) is a powerful financial and accounting tool to meet the needs of small to medium-sized organizations. From item inventories, quotes, sales and purchase orders, accounts payable and receivables, and general ledgers, GP can do it all. Can't find a solution you're looking for? There's a wealth of knowledge out in community forums, plus Microsoft offers great support. The GP community organizes a yearly summit where users ranging from accountants, DBAs, developers, and BI specialists get together and talk about the product, its latest features and updates, and custom integrations created by third-parties to make your life easier.

column7: <v ->Hi, my name is Erica, I'm a solar energy consultant.</v> I work in the solar industry, and I give Microsoft Dynamics 365 a five out of 10. Microsoft Dynamics 365, is a customer relations management system, and we use it for that. It's our way to communicate between the sales team, which is, I'm a part of, our operations team, our inside sales team, all aspects of the company to try to be on the same page for our clients. What I like best about Dynamics 365, is the ability for our whole team to communicate with one another. So, you know, from our inside sales team and they're setting appointments, and then we're able to review the information that they collected, and then we're also able to keep track of some notes during the sales process. And then once the project is sold, we can do the project management side. So, I like that we can communicate and see where are things are going without necessarily having to go back to each team member to find out exactly what's happening. It saves times, saves efficiency, for our crew, for sure, and our whole team. The other thing I like about it is the customization. I know that our, especially on our operations side, this seems to be more friendly for our operations side, but you would be able to customize it, and that's really helpful as well, so it looks like it's basically our software. What I dislike about Dynamics 365. A couple of things, I find it challenging to be able to do my job well with it. I think it's great for operations but a little more difficult for the sales team, and that's what I do, is sales. And one thing is that there's no task list, or activities, that I can assign to a certain day of the week, or a date, and then move it forward quickly, where I've had experience with other customer relationship management systems in the past, that did allow that. So instead, I have to actually got to my own system, and either have a paper handheld calendar, or use my Google calendar. Either way, it still requires me to do an extra step, and then I've gotta go back, and be in two screens, to look at what I'm referring to, in regard to the notes. So, it makes it a little more challenging to do my job. It's one of the most frustrating things actually, and reason why I gave it such a low score. Because in my position in sales, it's very challenging. The other thing that I find difficult, is you can't edit. So once you've made a note in the system, if for any reason you made a mistake, or you spelled something wrong, or all of a sudden you hit Enter on accident, you can't go back and edit that, and make a change to that particular note. So, it looks a little discombobulated and can be confusing to the operations team when they're trying to read it, like, wait, what's going on here? So those are a couple of things, the last one that just comes to mind right now, is just that it can be slow, and it can time out. And that gets really frustrating too, and I've just added a note, and it doesn't really work. Or, I have to go to an activity, and then I can go back and add a note, it's, there's just some glitches in it that, just lose efficiency, and I'm kinda big on efficiency and productivity. So, it wouldn't be my first choice for a customer relationship management system, from the sales point for our company, but it's what I get to work with, and I am trying to make the best of it. If you're considering Dynamics 365, some things that I would probably take a look at are, what, how, do you want to use Dynamics 365? Is it mainly for operations, is it mainly for your sales team, what type of communication do you want to occur? Personally, I have found it more beneficial in my sales roles, to use other companies, as I have in past companies, such as Salesforce, or Act!

column7: <v ->Hi my name is Allie,</v> I work in finance as a consultant, and I give Dynamics a eight out of ten. Dynamics is a program that is used at an enterprise level to manage data across multiple departments, and in this particular role, as a manager of accounting, it's important to know some of that detail and the issue that we run into specifically is being able to see the data in a raw format and also reporting easily without spending hours and hours building reports. I like Dynamics for its access to large amounts of data and the settings that you can put around the reporting process if you have the time available and you're willing to invest the time into the software it's well worth the time. What I dislike about Dynamics at this point in time as the manager, would be the time spent in setting up user profiles. If you're thinking about trying Dynamics for Microsoft you should probably check out the tutorials and the user guide, it's pretty in-depth, there are so many settings and it does take a lot of time to get it set up.

column7:
Dynamics GP has been refreshing to use in my accounting career!

The feature I used the most was the Smartlist search, where you could narrow down searches of the General Ledger. The feature is very robust to use and helped me to search for specific transactions over a long period of time. It is simple to use and can easily be exported into
column8:
We needed to use a consulting firm in order to integrate Dynamics GP with our ERP system. When I started the integration, I thought it would be for a one-time setup. I did not realize we would then need to buy an annual service contract to continue using our integration. Glad we did buy the contract though, because we needed to work on the integration for several months until we had a smooth process down.

Also, the built in trial balance reporting is not great. We used Management Reporter to get better financial reporting and Trial Balances.
column9:
Working for a regional retailer, we switched to Dynamics GP to use for our accounting and general ledger when we decided to switch our ERP system. The new ERP did not have an accounting piece built in properly, so our solution was to take an export from the ERP into Dynamics. The ERP was able to send out a basic daily pass off of sorts, that we then were able to import into Dynamics.

We were able to import batches every day, but unfortunately the ERP did not have very accurate accounting. At this time we are searching to see if we should use Dynamics for our ERP solution. Effective for Reporting in
column10: I would definitely use a consultant if you plan to switch to Dynamics GP. It's a large piece of software to manage.

column7: The best part about MS Dynamics GP is the ability to "copy and paste" journal entries into the General Journal entry window from any Excel workbook. The integration is as easy as highlighting an array of cells which contain a description, account, debit, and credit for each line item in the journal entry. I am also a very large fan of the smartlist feature. Smartlists basically let you build queries using their user-friendly interface. They will return anything (in tabular form) that doesn't require too complicated of join structures on the back end (database).
column8: Sometimes the software can run a bit slow. This may not be MS Dynamics GP's fault. Either way, it should be noted. For whatever reason, it can take a very long time for trial balances to generate. Also, the program can take a while to start up as well.
column9: MS Dynamics GP is accounting software. So, we are using it as our general ledger. The software makes sure the typical accounting controls are adhered to (debits = credits, user restrictions, etc.). The software appears to tackles all of these things sufficiently.

column7: I use Microsoft Dynamics for general ledger purposes, generating reports and financial statements. The reports can be exported to Excel but the out put is not very easy to use without editing. We use FRX report writer to achieve detailed financial statements from the data in Microsoft Dynamics.
column8: The account payable module in Microsoft Dynamics is complicated and not user friendly. Entering payables required creating a batch, entering each payable and then posting the batch. We stopped processing our corporate accounts payable through Microsoft Dynamics and now use Quickbooks for this function.

column7: Microsoft Dynamics NAV is an all encompassing source of data that is captured across the business. It allows various departments within the business to collaborate together at maximum efficiency by streamlining the flow of information and data.
column8: The system poorly integrates with other tools that we use, and thus a lot of data needs to be entered and/ or corrected by manual efforts that are both time consuming and frustrating.
column9:
TLC Marketing uses Microsoft Dynamics NAV as the primary Enterprise Resource Planning platform in our business.

It serves as our CRM system and client database. We also keep track of the sales team's and SDR rep's productivity via NAV and our revenue forecasting is based on data entered into the system.

Both the front end and the back-end of TLC Marketing rely on Microsoft Dynamics NAV to keep track of production flows and profitability.
column10:
As a CRM system, it has it's shortcomings although from what I've been told the new versions of Microsoft Dynamics 365 integrate nicely with Microsoft Dynamics NAV. Our company so far has not yet decided to upgrade that part of our system.

As an enterprise resource planning system, it certainly ticks all the boxes and safely delivers.

The biggest challenge I see is poor user experience across different interfaces within Dynamics, that end up harming the overall usefulness of the platform. 

The problem, as with any database, is that it's usefulness diminishes dramatically when users don't capture data correctly. column7:
My review comes from the perspective of an IT professional who works for a Microsoft Dynamics reseller, so I guess I have a certain amount of bias. That being said, I have been using Microsoft Dynamics NAV and here is what I like best:

Firstly, it is really easy for the developers to work on. They have access to all the source code of the system and a development environment is available out of the box. Additionally, there is an entire module of the system (RapidStart) devoted to helping the IT professionals migrate data into your system during your primary implementation. Additionally, from a developer's perspective, there are a lot of pre-packaged functions in development environment making it quick to do development that might take a bit longer in other programming languages.

Secondly, it is very easy to get data from the NAV system by creating queries/pages and oData feeds. These oData feeds will allow you to access your data from Excel PowerPivot, PowerBI, or other similar tools.

Thirdly, I like that the user interface is very easy to personalize (i.e. add and remove action buttons, fields, navigation buttons, etc. and create personal views of data to see later) and also your IT department can add and remove fields, pages, etc. from user interface for entire groups of users. Additionally, elements of user interface are automatically removed based on your license configuration and permissions of users.

Fourthly, in general the user interface is very attractive with large nice-looking icons, tiles to direct users to the tasks they need to do each day, etc.

Fifthly, as I understand it, NAV offers very good value compared to other systems. It is fast to implement and offers wide functionality.

Fifthly, Microsoft has a roadmap for this solution and is releasing updates every month and a major release every year (which is good). They have also done a lot to make the solution easy to upgrade.
column8:
Firstly, I think the charts feature in NAV is not as great as some other solutions I have used (but they make up for this by allowing you to embed a PowerBI dashboard into your system in latest version of NAV).

Secondly, the workflows feature is not as good as in Microsoft Dynamics CRM (then again it may be better than other ERP systems, plus it is possible to integrate CRM and NAV together which is nice).

Thirdly, sometimes it is challenging to understand the link between the various types of ledger entries in NAV, but this might simply be a challenge in any ERP system. It sometimes is challenging for example to reconcile the G/L with inventory ledger if you are using any of the features such as automatic cost posting to G/L, automatic cost adjustment, and expected cost posting. Additionally, in some versions it is not so easy to reverse invoices, but they have made up for this in latest version by allowing to cancel or adjust invoices (while still keeping a complete audit trail).

column7: Microsoft Dynamics GP has a simple, easy to use interface. The menus are easily customized to your liking. GP 2015 has an easy to use paste function for importing journal entries. Exporting reports to excel is a nice feature. I bet there are more workflow features that my team would find useful, but we don't really have much time to explore the software, we just kind of use it for immediate needs. Being able to change the printer after selecting the option to print has been huge. It took them way to long to add this feature!
column8: The reporting out of Microsoft Dynamics GP could be more robust. Getting to basic information takes more steps than it should. I wish there were more shortcuts so you could complete basic recurring tasks faster. I also wish switching between companies within GP was faster.
column9: Microsoft Dynamics GP is a great basic solution to our GL accounting needs. It doesn't take much training to get users started w/ the basic knowledge to complete their assigned tasks. Great
column10: This is a great basic solution for GL accounting needs, I recommend it.

column7: Dynamics GP has a very intuitive user interface that allows transactions to be processed quickly and easily. It can be installed and up and running relatively quickly and is flexible to scale from the smallest client to a rather large client pretty easily.
column8: Security can be a challenge - it's not the most intuitive product when you want to set up specific roles or add security functionality to roles or users. It also doesn't offer a way to easily flow from one business process to the next - some additional workflow options would be nice.
column9: The benefits of using GP and problems solved are that it allows for much greater flexibility and scaling as a business grows than a product like Quickbooks does but it's not as complicated as some of the other ERPs out there. Dynamics
column10: I would recommend becoming a member of GPUG (GP user group) community as there is a ton of useful information out there to be had and lots of users in the community that can share experiences. Also, I would recommend that you find an experienced partner to help you implement as there are certain settings that need to be looked at carefully upon initial implementation.

column7: It works consistently and reliably. Enormous functional capabilities aside I feel like accounting systems need to be accurate, consistent, and work all the time. It also has an incredibly deep ecosystem of third party applications that extend the base functionality of GP. You can't do it in GP? There's an app for that.
column8: Dexterity. I mean, come on already, it's 2016. I guess I should temper my lack of love for Dexterity with my previous comment because, after all, it's ancient code that still works after all these years but it doesn't lend itself to any real native development on the application itself. Integration it's fine, but it's a bit of a developmental black box.
column9: Accounting, finance and operational reporting. Prof Services management. The benefit we've realized from using it as the transactional/informational backbone of our entire business is that it drives consistency and compliance. We also have it integrated to Dynamics CRM and our Field Service Management and Time tracking applications.
column10: Give it a good look. This is not your father's "Great Plains". Dynamics GP 2013 and above, especially GP 2015 and 2016 is legit mid-market competition for Oracle, SAP, Infor, etc... in the mid-market without the price, complexity, and overhead of those products. Ever upgraded Oracle? If you have you know what I'm talking about.

column7: Microsoft has gone above and beyond with Dynamics GP. We are currently on release 2013 and came from version 8.0. I have setup Dynamics GP from scratch in different version and I can tell you that MS has improved GP for the better in each version over time. They are listening to the customer instead of just doing what they think is best for various industries. Customer opinions matter when it comes to this product and you can see this in the newest version, GP 2015.
column8:
Too many 3rd party apps to do what you want. I believe GP should include more functionality supported directly from Microsoft. I understand why they don't have certain products and MS can't support everything out there, but they should pull the top 3 or so products that customers use as a whole and consider making them part of the core product on a 3 year basis. 

Also, Microsoft products should work with MS products! I've seen a few 'workarounds' to get GP and other various MS products to "talk" together when they should already be doing this. There also has been no MS GP 64 bit version at this point and 64 bit technology / software has been out for some time now.
column9: We have been able to centralize many of our sites billing into our corporate office giving us more control over billing and vendor invoicing. This enabled us to do cost analysts and save money for our company. We were able to save money by combining various vendors and provide better solutions to our customers.
column10: Highly recommended to hire or train someone to become your 'in-house' MS GP expert. You will save a lot of money overtime getting someone in house hired or trained to be the GP expert in your business. External consultants are expensive and add up pretty quickly over time. Plus, you are also subject to be on their time which can delay projects. Having your own in-house expert will make a huge different in implementation and support when something goes wrong.

column7:
- I found that Dynamics GP had a lot of online training materials regarding the product.
- Very simple user creation.
- Uses SQL Server which is very easy to query. 
- Customization using the Dexterity library. 
- Report writer is a very robust tool, it makes it very easy to build and customize reports. 
- Newer versions of Dynamics GP uses SSRS to deploy and send reports, this makes it very easy for management to keep track of daily statistics. 
- I like how they did away with FRx for the drill down reports, it was a good move by Microsoft to integrate the drill down reports directly into Dynamics GP.
- The amount of information on customer / partner source.
column8:
- There are way too many 3rd party applications that integrate with Dynamics GP. They really could corner the market if they would bring all of these apps in house. Some of these applications are home based and run / developed and supported by a single person. (example: e=mc2)
- The same interface and admin options since the beginning, they really need to make a customization admin window to customize what the users see. Allow them to set the colors of Dynamics GP.
- Update the dexterity library to include entity framework libraries.
- The error messages can be very convoluted, they should be easily read by the end users.
- The customersource network should come with chat/remote support. The current ticketing system takes too long to get things together.
- Lack of integration with Active Directory, Dynamics GP uses SQL Server authenticated accounts.

column7: This is a rock solid, enterprise level ERP system that has been a great fit for our medium sized company (450 million gross revenue, 4300 employees). We selected GP in 2005 and went live shortly after in 2nd quarter 2006. Like any ERP, there will be a learning curve - especially if you're coming from an ancient legacy system like we were. And like in any product selection, be sure to spend plenty of time in the development of your implementation plan. What I like best about GP is hard to single out - so I'll pick 3 things. First, I like how intuitive it can be to use. Accounting screens (GL, AP, AR, etc) are straight forward, feature rich, and easy to use. Second, I have also been very happy with their support. Third, there are literally hundreds of 3rd party "add-on" products to further enhance GP beyond the core functionality. In my experience, any ERP system that suggests it is the single turnkey solution for an organization isn't being honest (there are a few exceptions for this - some ERPs are specifically targeted at certain markets - like retail, restaurants, etc.)
column8: Like any product, there are downsides. However, many of the things I don't like are features / functions they never promised were there in the first place. For example, there is no way to deal with intercompany AR (without purchasing a 3rd party module) - a feature so critical to any multi-company organization that I'm really surprised it was never developed. Although their human tech support is fantastic, their CustomerSource database is very difficult to search / navigate. And I find their GP2013 web portal to be horribly complicated and incredibly confusing.
column9: Our reason to selecting Dynamics GP was simply to replace an outdated, AS400 based accounting system that had originally been installed in 1982. We used this opportunity to update our GL chart of accounts, clean up historic data, rebuild / reengineer our financial reporting, and leverage the SQL database engine - enabling us to leverage accounting data with other internal systems we've built. Microsoft Dynamics
column10: Be sure to fully develop a project plan / scope document prior to project kick off. If you don't have these planning skills in house, hire a consultant with GP Dynamics experience. DO NOT simply pick someone who has simply worked with ERP's in general - insist on direct GP experience

column7: Dynamics GP is a powerful accounting package based on the popular Microsoft SQL Server platform. You are granted full access to the tables behind GP allowing for almost limitless possibilities for integration and custom reporting.
column8: It is a big leap from other smaller accounting suites like QuickBooks to GP. The modular based accounting platform is difficult for some people to wrap their heads around which creates a larger learning curve before staff are comfortable with the software. It works best in companies with an accounting department with staff trained in accounting principles.
column9: Accounting functions are the basic needs solved by the software, but it is ideal for those seeking high levels of integration and customization. Almost anything in the software can be customized with a little bit of work.
column10: GP is definitely designed as an on-premise software with weak online offerings. Implementation is often costly as you will typically need the help of a Microsoft Partner to set the software up and customize it. That said, there is so much available in terms of customization and integration that if you have a unique company model, many outside programs such as CRMs or sales software, or a unique industry, this is may be a good fit for you.

column7: Microsoft NAV is not an out-of-the-box product made to fit a every type of business. You need to have a direction and strategy of what you need in an ERP, and Microsoft NAV can deliver it. It can be tailored to meet your specific business needs for accounting, reporting, order management, and thru the implementation of ISV - retail, pos, and ecommerce. The ERP package we utilized prior to our switch was an "out-of-the-box" software package, and we fit our business into the box. In order for our company to grow we found value in an ERP that could be leverage to grow with us.
